chunks.text: \chapter{How To Create or Edit a File} \index{file!creating} \index{file!editing} \renewcommand{\thempfootnote}{\arabic{mpfootnote}} \setcounter{mpfootnote}{0} \setcounter{footnote}{0} Three editors, {\bf Emacs},\index{Emacs editor} {\bf JOVE}\inde

, {\bf Emacs},\index{Emacs editor} {\bf JOVE}\index{JOVE editor}, and {\bf vi}\index{vi editor} can be used to create files on the EECS Instructional machines. You can also create short files using the UNIX\TM\ {\bf cat} command. \begin{description}

he UNIX\TM\ {\bf cat} command. \begin{description} \item[vi:] {\bf vi} is the standard editor on UNIX\TM-based systems. \item[Emacs:] GNU Emacs\index{GNU {\bf Emacs}|see{{\bf Emacs} editor}} is becoming the most popular editor. Emacs is a public-do

ing the most popular editor. Emacs is a public-domain editor that is available for a variety of systems and is therefore popular with users who work on more than one system. Features available in Emacs help make debugging code with gdb (the GNU deb

acs help make debugging code with gdb (the GNU debugger) easier to manage. \item[JOVE:] {\bf JOVE} stands for ``Jonathon's Own Version of Emacs''. It is a modified version of Emacs that contains most of the important functionality, but not all the f

of the important functionality, but not all the features, of Emacs. \item[cat:] To create a file using the UNIX\TM\ {\bf cat} command,\index{cat@{\bf cat} command} first enter {\bf cat} {\tt >} {\em filename}, then enter the lines of text, pressing

filename}, then enter the lines of text, pressing \key{Return} after each line; end by pressing \ctrl{d}. If you make mistakes entering the lines, you'll probably need to use an editor to correct them. \end{description} \noindent Points to rememb

em. \end{description} \noindent Points to remember when editing files: \begin{description} \item[Find the control keys:] Before entering an editor, be sure that you know where to find the the special control keys, such as \key{Ctrl}, \key{Meta} an

al control keys, such as \key{Ctrl}, \key{Meta} and \key{Escape}.\index{editing!control keys} \item[Enter commands in the correct case:] Remember that UNIX\TM\ is case sensitive. \item[Save your file frequently:] When you save your edited file, the

e frequently:] When you save your edited file, the original version is gone. (In fact, Emacs makes a copy of the original, but JOVE and vi don't.) Until you are familiar with UNIX\TM\ and the editor you are using, you may wish to make an extra copy

you are using, you may wish to make an extra copy of your file before you start to edit it. For example, if you want to edit a filed named {\it mywork}, you could do: \begin {quote} {\tt \%} {\bf cp mywork myworkBackUp} \end{quote} before doing a

mywork myworkBackUp} \end{quote} before doing any editing. Editors usually create temporary files from which recovery {\it may} be possible in case of a system crash (or in case you inadvertently close the window in which you were doing the editi

close the window in which you were doing the editing). However, it isn't wise to depend on recovering a file in this way. So {\it save your file frequently} during your editing session. Also, save different versions of files --- don't just save i

different versions of files --- don't just save it with the same name over and over again, because if you accidentally delete huge portions of the file and wipe out your only copy, you are stuck. Consider naming files {\tt mywork.1}, {\tt mywork.2}

nsider naming files {\tt mywork.1}, {\tt mywork.2}, etc. Delete old copies when you are sure you have a good one. You may need to set the terminal type so that the editor will display to your screen correctly and recognize your control characters.

correctly and recognize your control characters. See Section~\ref{termtype}. If you cannot save your file in your account for any reason (like the disk quota system won't let you), you can save your files {\it temporarily} in /tmp. /tmp is a te

our files {\it temporarily} in /tmp. /tmp is a temporary directory that all users can write into. Your files may only stay here a short time and are not guaranteed to remain there, but you can usually store files there while we resolve your disk st

ly store files there while we resolve your disk storage problem. \end{description} \section{Using the Emacs or JOVE Editors} \index{Emacs editor} \index{JOVE editor} \label{jove} GNU Emacs is a complicated editor with a large number of sophisticat

plicated editor with a large number of sophisticated features. The purpose of this section is to provide you with enough information to create and edit files. JOVE is a simpler modified version of the Emacs editor. Many of the commands are similiar

he Emacs editor. Many of the commands are similiar, but there are differences in some of the command syntax and not all Emacs commands are available in JOVE. We have tried to clarify these differences when relevent to the commands listed in this cha

s when relevent to the commands listed in this chapter. \subsection{Emacs Windows} When you use {\bf Emacs} on a workstation, it creates a window for you to work in. (JOVE just runs in whatever window you start it in.) If you don't like the size

dow you start it in.) If you don't like the size or position of the window, you can change it by resizing the window. The window is divided into two parts by a black bar containing status information. The large area above the bar is the buffer-ed

on. The large area above the bar is the buffer-editing area; the small area, usually a single line below the bar is the echo area or minibuffer where Emacs displays messages and prompts for responses (see Section~\ref{emacs:buffers}). \subsection{T

(see Section~\ref{emacs:buffers}). \subsection{Temporary Files} \index{Emacs editor!temporary files} If you exit from Emacs normally and save your file, you will find that Emacs has also saved your original file; the filename for this old version

original file; the filename for this old version is formed by appending a tilde ({\tt\~{ }}) to the original filename. Every 300 characters, Emacs saves the changes in an auto-save\index{Emacs editor!auto-save} file. The auto-save filename is form

r!auto-save} file. The auto-save filename is formed by adding a pound sign ({\tt\#}) at the beginning and end of the original filename. If you exit from Emacs abnormally, you will see this file. You can recover\index{Emacs editor!recovering edits}

u can recover\index{Emacs editor!recovering edits} most of your edits from this file in case of a crash or in case you inadvertently close the Emacs window without exiting from Emacs. It isn't wise to count on this auto-save file, though; it's bette

o count on this auto-save file, though; it's better to get in the habit of saving your file every five or ten minutes. You can do this without exiting from Emacs. Since these Emacs temporary files are created automatically, you need to check your a

re created automatically, you need to check your account periodically to be sure you don't accumulate temporary files and exceed your disk quota. JOVE's behavior with temporary files is different. JOVE temporary files have names like jreca13016 and

OVE temporary files have names like jreca13016 and jovea13016 and get deleted when you exit JOVE normally. If you don't exit JOVE normally (due to a system crash, modem hang, etc.), you will need to delete these files manually. \subsection{Invoking

delete these files manually. \subsection{Invoking Emacs or JOVE} You can invoke Emacs or JOVE by entering one of the UNIX\TM\ commands {\bf emacs}, {\bf emacs} {\em filename}, {\bf jove}, or {\bf jove} {\em filename}. On workstations, it is more us

ve} {\em filename}. On workstations, it is more useful to invoke Emacs with the {\bf emacs \&} command because then you can move back and forth between your UNIX\TM\ window and your Emacs window.\footnote{You cannot do ``{\bf jove \&}'' to get anothe

note{You cannot do ``{\bf jove \&}'' to get another window. If you want a separate JOVE window, you must open up a new window and run jove in the new window.} This allows you to enter UNIX\TM\ commands without exiting from Emacs---just move the mou

nds without exiting from Emacs---just move the mouse cursor to the window you want to use. In either case, be sure to save your file and exit the editor before you logoff. \subsection{Entering Text and Commands} In Emacs and JOVE, you are either t

and Commands} In Emacs and JOVE, you are either typing text or entering commands. Commands always start with a prefix character, either the Control key \key{CTRL}\index{control key (\key{CTRL})} or a meta key \key{META}.\index{metacharacter key (\ke

meta key \key{META}.\index{metacharacter key (\key{META})} Press and hold down this prefix character while typing the character that completes the command. On workstation keyboards, the \key{Compose Character} key\index{Compose Character key (\key{

Character} key\index{Compose Character key (\key{Compose Character})} is the \key{Meta} key. On other keyboards, it might be \key{Edit}\index{Edit key (\key{Edit})} or \key{Alt}.\index{Alt key (\key{Alt})} If you can't find your \key{Meta} key or y

{Alt})} If you can't find your \key{Meta} key or your keyboard doesn't have one, use \key{Esc}.\index{Escape key (\key{Esc})} If you do use \key{Esc}, release it before typing the character that completes the command. If you type a closing parenth

pletes the command. If you type a closing parenthesis, square bracket, or curly brace in the text you are entering, the cursor will momentarily move to the corresponding opening parenthesis, bracket or brace. This helps you see whether you are matc

or brace. This helps you see whether you are matching them correctly. Note that this is a mode in Emacs which can be turned off if you don't wish to use it. In JOVE, this mode is automatically on if the file you're editing has a ``.c'', ``.l'', or

the file you're editing has a ``.c'', ``.l'', or ``.scm'' extension, i.e., ``homework.scm''. \subsection{Common Emacs and JOVE Commands} \index{Emacs editor!commands} \index{JOVE editor!commands} Tables~\ref{emacs:cmds} lists commonly-used Emacs a

ables~\ref{emacs:cmds} lists commonly-used Emacs and JOVE commands. \label{emac-comm} When a command requires a response of some kind, for example a filename or a string, you will be prompted for it in the echo area at the bottom of the screen. Ent

in the echo area at the bottom of the screen. Enter your response and press \key{Return}. When you need to enter a filename, the prompt will include the current path. If the file is in this path, just enter the filename. If it isn't, back up over t

st enter the filename. If it isn't, back up over the path and enter the correct path and filename. If you change your mind in the middle of a command and wish to cancel that command, \ctrl{g} will cancel the command you're in the middle of and retur

ered, save it. & \ctrl{x} \ctrl{s} \\ & Recover JOVE auto-save file (from UNIX) & {\bf jove -r}\\ & Cancel the current command & \ctrl{g} \\ \end{tabular} \end{table} \subsection{Emacs/JOVE Buffers}\label{emacs:buffers

subsection{Emacs/JOVE Buffers}\label{emacs:buffers} \index{Emacs editor!buffers} When you use Emacs or JOVE to create or modify a file, a temporary workspace called a buffer is set up for you. Your file is edited in the buffer and the original file

ile is edited in the buffer and the original file is not modified until you ask to save the changes. You can edit more than one buffer at a time if you wish. Some commands also create a new buffer---Emacs/JOVE will either divide your window into tw

-Emacs/JOVE will either divide your window into two parts, for example, when you ask for help, or will hide your editing buffer, replacing it with the new area. To get back to your main buffer, you need to use one of the buffer/window commands. If

eed to use one of the buffer/window commands. If you get into trouble with buffers and can't figure out what to do, enter \ctrl{x} \ctrl{c} to exit from the editor. You will be asked if you want to save the file you are editing (unless you are edit

save the file you are editing (unless you are editing it in the Scratch buffer).\index{Emacs editor!exiting} \subsection{Learning More About Emacs} Online help for Emacs includes a tutorial. Look at Table~\ref{emac-comm} for how to access the online

Table~\ref{emac-comm} for how to access the online help. While you are learning Emacs and aren't familiar with moving between Emacs buffers, it is probably simpler to look at help when you are not editing a file. Then you can just exit Emacs without

ting a file. Then you can just exit Emacs without trying to figure out how to get back to your editing buffer. \subsection{Learning More About JOVE}\label{teachjove}\index{teachjove} For an on-line tutorial on JOVE, run the command {\bf teachjove}.

utorial on JOVE, run the command {\bf teachjove}. \footnote{If you get an error message ``{\tt Command not found}'', you may have to ask your system adminstrator (``root'') where this is installed.} \subsection{Trouble in Emacs or JOVE}\label{ctrlS}

\subsection{Trouble in Emacs or JOVE}\label{ctrlS} Depending upon your system or local configuration of Emacs or JOVE, you may run into a problem where \ctrl{s} freezes the screen instead of performing a text search. If this happens, type \ctrl{q} t

g a text search. If this happens, type \ctrl{q} to ``unfreeze'' your screen. \section{Using the {\bf vi} Editor}\index{vi editor} Even if you plan to use another editor, you may want to know a little about the {\bf vi} editor because it is the sta

le about the {\bf vi} editor because it is the standard editor on UNIX\TM\ systems. As do most editors, {\bf vi} edits your file in a buffer and doesn't modify the original file until you ask it to. \subsection{Entering Text and Commands} Since vi

\subsection{Entering Text and Commands} Since vi commands are just letters like `i' and `x', you need to tell vi whether you are entering text or commands. When you enter vi, you are in Command mode, and vi assumes that everything you enter is a c

e, and vi assumes that everything you enter is a command. When you want to enter text, you need to enter a command to switch to Input mode. Vi then assumes that everything you enter is text until you press \key{Escape} to return to Command mode. I

u press \key{Escape} to return to Command mode. In addition to Command and Input modes, vi also has a Line mode. \begin{description} \item[Command mode:] When you enter vi, you are in Command mode. In this mode you can move the cursor, enter vi com

In this mode you can move the cursor, enter vi commands, save your file, and exit from the editor.\index{vi editor!command mode} \item[Input mode:] In order to enter text, you need to switch to Input mode.\index{vi editor!input mode} Then you enter

mode.\index{vi editor!input mode} Then you enter the text and return to Command mode by pressing the \key{Escape} key. While in Input mode, you can enter several lines of text, pressing \key{Return} after each line, but you cannot move the cursor u

after each line, but you cannot move the cursor until you \key{Escape} back to command mode. % (This irritates people who are accustomed to being able to %move the cursor and enter text without changing modes, but you get %used to it fairly rapidly.

ing modes, but you get %used to it fairly rapidly.) \item[Line mode:] From command mode, you can also issue line commands\index{vi editor!line mode} to delete or change a range of lines, execute UNIX\TM\ commands, and exit from the editor. You sta

\TM\ commands, and exit from the editor. You start these commands with a colon (``{\bf :}'') and end them by pressing \key{Return}. \end{description} \subsection{Invoking vi}\index{vi editor!invoking} Before you invoke vi, you should know where

ing} Before you invoke vi, you should know where your \key{Esc}\index{Escape key (\key{Esc})} key is. On many workstations, it is \key{F11}; on some terminals, it is labeled \key{Esc}. If you don't know where the \key{Escape} is, you can't get out

know where the \key{Escape} is, you can't get out of input mode and you won't be able to exit from vi normally. You can invoke vi with {\bf vi} or {\bf vi}~{\it filename}. \subsection{Common vi Commands}\index{vi editor!commands} Table~\ref{vi:cmd

IX\TM\ command & {\bf :!} {\em UNIX\TM\ command} \\ \end{tabular} \end{table} \subsection{Trouble in vi}\index{vi editor!problems} Vi on many systems saves a temporary file containing your edits. If the system crashes while you are e

your edits. If the system crashes while you are editing a file, you may be able to recover most of what you were doing, but don't count on it. Save your file frequently while you are editing. To try to recover edits from this temporary file, enter
y to recover edits from this temporary file, enter the command ``{\bf vi -r} {\em filename}'' \index{vi editor!file recovery} where the filename is the one you were editing before the crash.
